data.title: ECAT: Saba's Activism Drives Double-Digit Yield, Upcoming Vote Is Key
data.description:
BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Term Trust (NYSE:ECAT) is a closed-end fund with roughly $1.6 billion in assets under management and trading at a 4.37% discount to its Net Asset Value (NAV). ECAT is a very interesting situation because of its huge 22.84% distribution yield and an upcoming shareholder vote. This vote is about a slate of directors put forward by shareholder Saba Capital and whether BlackRock should remain in place as manager. Even if only the director's Saba put forward are voted in, it could ultimately lead to BlackRock being cast aside as manager. As interesting as this is, I think this CEF is either a hold or a marginal buy right now.

I have a lot of confidence in the activist, Saba Capital. It is led by Boaz Weinstein, and he's been pressing BlackRock to better its corporate governance at all of its CEFs. Saba has been very successful with a number of funds, and ultimately, the two parties agreed to something of a truce. Except in regard to this fund. Many BlackRock CEFs traded at fairly deep discounts, especially given the brand, but have now greatly increased their distributions and instituted discount management programs or major tender offers(for 40% or 50% of shares).

ECAT utilizes a little bit of leverage and achieved an NAV return of 4.25% over the past year. It generated an annualized 5.70% since inception, while its market price total return was 10.13% over the past year and an average of 4.76% annualized since inception. These performance figures have been improved quite a bit by Saba. Pushing the discount from double digits towards low single digits. Ironically, Saba made its own life a little more difficult, by winning this upcoming shareholder vote.

I have previously written about other CEFs where Saba Capital has been active, and I’ve been very happy with their efforts to narrow discounts on BIGZ and BMEZ. The current situation with ECAT hinges on the crucial shareholder meeting vote on June 26, 2025. BlackRock could get eliminated as a manager directly. Or if Saba's directors get elected, it could lead to the termination of BlackRock advisors. At other CEFS like the Brandywine Global Income Opportunities Fund (BRW) and the Voya Prime Rate Trust (SABA) Saba has ultimately taken over management of these funds. However, Weinstein also recently retweeted (with the comment; "They get it") a Stifel report on U.K. investments trusts (the UK equivalent of CEFs) which said the following:

Possibly, this could all still go away if the fund is willing to tender for a substantial percentage of its outstanding shares. There's one reason why Saba may not be as willing to settle here, which I'll get into in a minute.

First, I'd like to say I doubt the current 20%+ distribution rate is ultimately sustainable. Most of this distribution is qualified as a return of capital. There’s nothing wrong with that. I like returns of capital as long as it’s clear that’s what they are. It makes total sense, too. Generating 20% annual returns with a mixed fund with only a dash of leverage is the stuff of legends. Not something to accept as a going-forward projection. The elevated distribution is still a great idea because the fund has over $1.5 billion of assets under management. If that’s trading at a double-digit discount(like it used to be) and it throws off of a 20% yield; there’s ~$30 million of value being created out of thin air (before taxes) every year.

ECAT currently trades at a much narrowed 4.37% discount. This is considerably improved from its 3-year average discount of approximately 10%. This is likely a direct effect of this high distribution rate and the instituted discount management program that magically appeared after Saba started prodding BlackRock. This narrowed discount is the key reason I’m not rating this a buy. At a higher discount, it would be much easier to justify.

Saba holds ~28.8% of ECAT (by the end of 2024). Other activist/CEF specialist firms like RiverNorth and Karpus also hold sizable positions. My (I presume conservative) estimate of the collective activist-oriented stake is that it's ~30%.

Now I'll get into why I believe Saba may be less willing to settle here. Saba also ran a slate of directors last year. After not being able to get its directors elected, Saba issued an incensed press release, calling BlackRock's behavior in that proxy fight hypocritical. Weinstein said:

In removing Saba’s votes and others who also voted for Saba's nominees from its 2024 Annual Meeting vote tally, BlackRock has slumped to a new low where anything goes – even outright deceit.

What I think he meant and implied later in that press release is that the way BlackRock has set up by laws of some of the funds isn’t fair. Apparently, BlackRock counts only the votes of people present (emphasis mine):

…BlackRock has created an unlevel playing field by manufacturing rigged elections that allow its hand-picked trustees to serve indefinitely. According to BlackRock’s rules, if 52 people show up to vote out of 100 registered voters, the winning party must receive support from 50 of them – or 96% of the votes. It's a fact that shareholders by a huge margin chose Saba’s nominees for BIGZ and ECAT…

Saba also included the 2024 vote tally showing their candidates got way more “For” votes but weren’t elected:

Saba and BlackRock have settled matters outside of ECAT. Over this one issue, they’re still going to court. I can imagine this is about a principle that Saba (and other activists) would like to see changed. With practices like this, it is very hard to enact change.

This makes for a complicated investment case, but I'll try to distill things down to a few simple scenarios:

1) Saba takes control (likely tender offer): If shareholders vote for the Saba slate, and ultimately Saba takes over, it makes sense the firm will implement the same strategies it does at Saba I and II. This is my preferred outcome for long-term value. However, it may not lead to an immediate further narrowing of the discount. In fact, it could be suboptimal in the short term given the current narrow discount. However, in past cases, Saba did do a tender near NAV to allow shareholders to get out once they were installed.

2) BlackRock/Saba still settles (tender offer): Another scenario could be a settlement involving a significant tender offer at a price very close to NAV. That's what happened at BIGZ and BMEZ. Given the firms settled on all other fronts except this, I'm inclined to believe they're looking for a court decision. However, if they do settle, it wouldn’t be far out, and it would potentially mean a ~2-4% uplift.

3) BlackRock wins proxy fight again: If this happens again, there are two sub-scenarios, A) Saba wins the court case arguing entrenchment practices are unfair, or B) Saba loses the court case. At best this results in a similar result as 1) and at worst Saba moves on, and the discount widens out quite a bit. The latter is the least favorable outcome. However, there will be value in these matters getting cleared up. Potentially to the benefit of all CEF shareholders.

Given the above scenarios, if I were an ECAT shareholder, I would be inclined to vote for Saba's directors. Boaz Weinstein has a great reputation for unlocking value in closed-end funds and as a world-class credit investor.

To be fair, I don’t think BlackRock is bad at all. Their track record with this fund, and some other CEFs, isn't great. However, these were launched relatively recently and performance isn't very indicative of skill over such short periods.

I often use and invest in BlackRock products. At the same time, it is a firm that’s especially good at gathering assets under management. Saba is a firm that’s focused much more on alpha generation or outstanding performance (as I see it). In the end, it seems myopic to look at the current discount and distribution of ECAT and conclude everything is fine here. Things are looking good because of Saba. If the activist is rebuffed or moves on, the shareholder-friendly programs could disappear over time.

data.title: CrowdStrike, GameStop To Report Earnings Next Week As Investors Focus On Job Openings And Unemployment Rate
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street on Friday posted its strongest monthly advance since November 2023, as markets recovered from the shock of U.S. President Donald Trump's April imposition of sweeping reciprocal tariffs. Friday's trading session ended mixed after Trump accused China of violating a trade truce. Back-and-forth over the legality of Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs grabbed the spotlight this week.

As investors turn their attention to June, next week will be quite busy in terms of economic data, Federal Reserve speakers and earnings. Monday will include reports on Global Manufacturing PMI and Construction Spending. Meanwhile, Fed chair Jerome Powell will give a speech at an event in Washington, D.C. Factory orders and Job openings are due on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of the Mortgage market index and Mortgage refinance index, with Imports and Exports, Initial Jobless Claims, and Trade balance on Thursday. Friday will include reports on Nonfarm payrolls and the Unemployment rate.

CrowdStrike (CRWD), Hewlett Packard (HPE), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), GameStop (GME), Broadcom (AVGO), Lululemon Athletica (LULU), and DocuSign (DOCU) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, June 2: Campbell Soup (CPB), Nano Dimension (NNDM). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, June 3: CrowdStrike, Hewlett Packard, Dollar General. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, June 4: Dollar Tree. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, June 5: Broadcom, Lululemon Athletica, DocuSign, Samsara (IOT), Rubrik (RBRK). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: LiveWire (LVWR) has a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include M3-Brigade Acquisition V. (MBAV), LZ Technology (LZMH), and SharpLink Gaming (SBET). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Champion Homes (SKY), Uni-Fuels (UFG), and Lionsgate Studios (LION). GameStop (GME) social media interactions have spiked since the company announced it bought bitcoin.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Principal Financial (PFG), Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN), Wendy's (WEN), Analog Devices (ADI), Western Digital (WDC), Qualcomm (QCOM), Booking (BKNG), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), and PepsiCo (PEP). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO watch: Circle (CRCL) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading in the week ahead. Circle is looking to raise about $624 million at a valuation around $6 billion on the New York Stock Exchange. ARK Investment Management and BlackRock (BLK) are expected to be early investors. The quiet period expires on Aspen Insurance (AHL) and American Integrity Insurance Group (AII) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Investor Events: The three-day Deutsche Bank dbAccess Global Consumer Conference will run from June 4-6, and will feature participation from major consumer firms such as P&G (PG), Kraft Heinz (KHC) and Kenvue (KVUE). Also of note will be William Blair's 45th Annual Growth Stock Conference from June 3-5, an event that was attended by nearly 800 investors in its 2024 edition.

CrowdStrike Earnings Preview: Cybersecurity company CrowdStrike is scheduled to report its first quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $0.66 on a revenue of $1.11B. RBC analysts recently expressed an optimistic view on software stocks for 2025 as recent checks on the software sector suggest stable demand trends despite ongoing macro or tariff uncertainty. In the cybersecurity segment, RBC increased the price target for CrowdStrike, citing continued success with cross-selling efforts.

Hewlett Packard Earnings Preview: Information technology company Hewlett Packard (HPE) is set to report its second quarter earnings next week, with the Street estimating earnings per share of $0.33 on a revenue of $7.5B. Evercore recently upgraded Hewlett Packard Enterprise's rating to Outperform from In Line and raised the price target on the shares to $22 from $17. Analysts said they view the current risk/reward as favorable, with multiple pathways to upside if investors have the duration. The analysts added that there has been a focus on several scenarios that could unfold at Hewlett Packard given the Juniper Networks (JNPR) transaction, and Elliott engagement.

data.title: Aya Gold & Silver: Mining Native Silver In Morocco
data.description:
Description

Aya Gold & Silver (TSX:AYA:CA) is a mid-tier and pure-play silver producer with all of its mining assets located in the Kingdom of Morocco. The company's flagship asset is the Zgounder Mine, located in the Anti-Atlas mountain range. With a production of 8.6 million ounces of silver in 2024, Morocco is the 16th largest silver producer in the world, but the largest on the African continent. The largest silver mine in the country is Imiter, owned by Managem, a mining group with operations in eight African countries and listed on the Casablanca Stock Exchange.

The Zgounder and Imiter mines are among a select class when it comes to ore quality and metallurgy, as some parts of their deposit contain native silver. In both cases, a proportion of the final product is in the form of 99.5% pure silver bars, which can result in much lower smelting or refining charges compared to products sold in the form of concentrate or doré bars. The further away the final product is from pure silver bullion, the higher the smelting charges are. It may look like a benign nuance, but as the numbers below illustrate, the impacts on operating margins can amount to a few percentage points.

Throughout 2024, silver concentrate and silver ingots represented approximately 70% and 30% of Aya's sales, respectively. However, with the new Zgounder mill ramping up, management expects that only silver ingots will be produced and sold starting in Q2-25. According to regulatory filings, Aya was paid approximately 85% of the metal value with concentrate sales. In contrast, based on management's comments during the Q1-25 conference call, Aya expects higher realized prices relative to average spot prices in a given quarter. The company's strategy is to hold bullion inventory in Geneva while patiently offering physical metal on the ask side of the market.

We are very choosy on how we sell our silver because we have liquidity, we're well funded, so we don't need to rush. So we build inventory in Geneva. We have the silver available for a transaction and we sell it normally by moving the market up. We will price it above market and we wait for the market to move up and come and hit, the bid to come and hit our offer and we're offering metal. It's not paper silver, it's metal, it's identified as such and we normally beat the average of the month or average of the quarter. So if you look at the average realized price by Aya and compare that to the average silver price of the quarter, we beat it steadily. And that is being very disciplined in how we sell it. Aya Gold & Silver Q1-25 Conference Call

For the full year of 2024, Aya reported treatment, smelting, and refining costs of $3.0 million, representing 7.6% of total revenues. If we look at the Q1-25 numbers, those same costs amounted to $0.5 million, or approximately 1.3% of total revenues. As illustrated below, ingots represented ~85% of total revenues in Q1-25, and with the Zgounder mill still ramping up, ingot sales as a proportion of total sales should increase further in the coming quarters.

To put Aya's metallurgy in perspective, MAG Silver (MAG) reported treatment, smelting, and refining costs of $35.3 million in 2024, or the equivalent of 5.6% of total sales. MAG discloses those numbers on a 100% basis, even though it owns a 44% interest in Juanicipio. As discussed in previous articles, the remaining 56% interest is owned by Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF), and MAG is unlikely to be traded for much longer with Pan American's (PAAS) buyout.

Fresnillo sells the totality of its production to Met-Mex for smelting, and refining. For the full year of 2024, Fresnillo recorded refining and treatment charges of $143.5 million, representing ~4% of the gross metal value. Notably, Fresnillo generates almost 60% of its revenues from the sale of various concentrates, which may partly explain why smelting charges look so high. Fresnillo: Juanicipio Standing Out Once Again

In short, the Zgounder mine is in a unique position when it comes to metallurgy. With a disciplined marketing strategy, Aya's realized prices should be slightly above average spot prices over a given period. On top, I would not be surprised if treatment, smelting and refining costs drop below 1.0% of sales in the coming quarter.

As illustrated below, Aya is the only pure-play silver miner that I am aware of. Fresnillo, MAG, and Endeavour (EXK) also offer exposure to silver, although it is diluted by a meaningful exposure to gold. As of today, I know very little about Silvercorp Metals (SVM), but I plan to research the company over the next few days.

Zgounder Mill Expansion

After the new Zgounder mill reached commercial production in December 2024, the mine produced 1.1 million ounces of silver in the first quarter of 2025. Following the earnings release, Aya's stock sold off sharply as cash costs came in at $18.93 per ounce, which is much higher than the all-in sustaining costs of $9.58 per ounce expected in the Feasibility Study released in February 2022. During the quarter, Aya encountered issues with the oxygen plant, which was operating below capacity, resulting in lower-than-expected recoveries. Labour costs should also diminish over the coming quarters after the mill ramp-up is completed.

What impresses me the most about the Zgounder Project is its achievement on a $140 million budget. According to management, the price tag for a similar project could have been between $400 million and $500 million in North America. The regulatory and permitting processes would have been much longer, too. The pattern would also have been similar when it comes to labour and energy costs.

With low initial capital requirements and production costs, the payback period is expected to be around one year, with an after-tax internal rate of return estimated at 75% using a realistic silver price of $28.00 per ounce. As illustrated below, the project's after-tax IRR increases to 117%, and the payback period diminishes to just a few months using a silver price assumption of $36.00 per ounce.

With an average annual production of ~6.8 million ounces over an 11-year mine life, the Zgounder Project is of similar size to Endeavour's Terronera Project, which entered wet commissioning in early May 2025. Once at full production, Terronera is expected to produce ~7.0 million ounces of equivalent silver after capital expenditures of around $230 million. Using a silver price assumption of $26.00 per ounce, Terronera's IRR is estimated at 34.6%, so about half of what is expected from Zgounder.

Once Zgounder reaches full production, Aya will also approach MAG in terms of production volume. For the full year of 2024, Juanicipio produced 23.0 million ounces of silver equivalent, of which 44% or 10.1 million ounces was attributable to MAG's shareholders.

In any case, the economic parameters from Zgounder's feasibility study certainly look great, but it is now time for Aya to support those numbers with tangible cash flows.

Boumadine Project

The second-largest asset in Aya's portfolio is an 85% interest in the Boumadine Project, also located in the Kingdom of Morocco. The remaining 15% interest is owned by the Office national des hydrocarbures et des mines, also known as ONHYM, a public institution with the mandate of developing natural resources inside the country's borders. Between 1950 and 1992, historical underground mining operations occurred on the property.

Over the last few years, Aya deployed a meaningful amount of capital to advance exploration on the property, notably with a 76,000-meter drilling program in 2023, followed by a 108,000-meter program in 2024. According to the company's guidance, another ~120,000-meter drilling program is planned for 2025. As illustrated below, Boumadine's drilling and other exploration expenses amounted to $14.8 million and $31.6 million in 2023 and 2024, respectively.

After investing almost $50 million in exploration over the last two years or so, Aya reported an updated mineral resource estimate on the project in February 2025. The revised estimates include 74.4 million silver-equivalent ounces in the indicated category and 377.7 million silver-equivalent ounces in the inferred category. Whether it is in the indicated or inferred categories, silver itself accounts for only 20% of total silver equivalent ounces, highlighting Boumadine's meaningful exposure to gold, lead and zinc.

Aya has done a good job growing Boumadine's resources, but considering the project's early-stage nature and the amount of capital deployed towards exploration, reinvestment risks are real. Once in full production, Zgounder will generate meaningful free cash flows, of which a significant proportion will be reinvested in the Boumadine Project.

On a personal note, I find the Zgounder Mine highly attractive to gain exposure to spot silver prices. If the numbers included in the feasibility study can be reproduced in the real world, Zgounder would become my second favourite asset in the silver mining industry, after Fresnillo's Juanicipio and in front of Endeavour's Terronera mine. However, my main source of skepticism surrounding Aya is capital allocation, and more precisely, the reinvestment of Zgounder's free cash flow into an early-stage exploration property.

Conclusion

Benoit La Salle, Aya's president and chief executive officer, had prior success in West Africa after founding SEMAFO in 1995. The company evolved from a junior explorer to a gold miner producing over 250,000 ounces per year. In August 2012, after the announcement of operational challenges at the Mana Mine in Burkina Faso, Mr. La Salle announced that he was stepping down from his role as president and CEO. SEMAFO's story ended in March 2020 when Endeavour Mining (EDV:CA) announced an all-stock buyout offer valued at CAD$1.0 billion.

Just a few weeks later, in April 2020, Mr. La Salle was appointed as Aya's president and CEO. Following Aya's reorganization and SEMAFO's buyout, many former SEMAFO executives joined Aya's team. In a way, Aya's story in Morocco could be seen as a sequel to SEMAFO's story in West Africa, a kind of SEMAFO 2.0. However, I was surprised to discover that Mr. La Salle owns only ~60,000 shares of the company he leads, which is quite small relative to his 2.0 million stock options and ~360,000 RSUs.

On top of a generous compensation, Aya also disclosed in its most recent financial statement the payment of management and consulting fees of approximately $100,000 per month to Group La Salle, a company owned by Mr. La Salle.

When we look at Aya from the standpoint of minority shareholders, the picture is slightly different, as they receive no compensation for sitting on the board of directors, no stock options, no management and consulting fees and no salary. In fact, the only way for minority shareholders to make money is via a higher share price or through dividend payments. At the end of the day, what kind of power can small shareholders like you and me have?

Unfortunately, with Boumadine's early-stage nature and the large capital requirements associated with its development, minority shareholders may not fully taste the free cash flows from Zgounder. I feel like it is often the case with mining; minority shareholders rarely taste the fruits of their investment. When something is indeed successful, the money is poured back into other projects. The problem is that we all know how difficult it is to compound capital in mining. Instead of compounding, I would prefer a model of redistribution, and I will take care of the compounding myself. In today's market, this strategy seems increasingly rare, or even non-existent.

For the company itself and for the people in positions of control, it may make perfect sense to reinvest Zgounder's free cash flows into an early-stage exploration project. In fact, developing the precious metal mining industry in Morocco may be part of a deal with the local government to obtain permits. Even if no deal of this kind were signed, what message would it send to Morocco's government if Aya were to return most or even all of Zgounder's cash flows back to its foreign shareholders?

My 'Hold' rating reflects my optimism regarding Zgounder's free cash flow generation, but also my concerns about the reinvestment risks associated with the Boumadine Project. As a minority shareholder seeking exposure to silver prices, I want to minimize exploration and development risks as much as possible. At the end of the day, there are no agency risks associated with owning the metal itself; there is no management team between my capital and the assets purchased. Before accepting the presence of a third party in the equation, I want to make sure our financial interests are aligned.

I will continue to follow Aya's story closely in the upcoming quarters. Before upgrading my 'Hold' rating, I would have to gain additional conviction on Boumadine's near-term development potential. Additionally, Zgounder still has to replicate in the real world the economic parameters included in the project's feasibility study. It is time to show in the real world what was on paper just a few quarters ago.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Income Or Safety? I Demand Both
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

We’ve all faced it – standing in front of the freezer, torn between two ice cream flavors, and deciding to get a sundae with a perfect mix of both. Or debating between two car models, each with features you like, but wondering if there was a third one offering the best of both.

Investing brings similar dilemmas. You want reliable income, but multiple sectors seem equally compelling. Sometimes, you don’t have to choose, and can have both. You can use the fund wrapper to unlock new mixed possibilities or reach sectors that can

be restrictive without an excessive amount of cash on hand. Closed-end funds, or CEFs, come in many shapes and varieties, designed to help investors achieve the perfect mix for their risk tolerance and appetite.

Today, we’re looking at a CEF that blends the strengths of two powerful income-generating asset classes – preferred securities and REITs – into one investment. Both sectors stand to benefit greatly from interest rate cuts, are relatively immune to trade tensions, and tend to remain resilient during recessions. We'll follow it up with a CEF that dives into a sector where holding a lot of variety can be restrictive and expensive, but this fund gives you the ability to do just that.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: RNP – Yield 7.5%

Cohen & Steers REIT and Preferred Income Fund (RNP) has ~$1.5 billion in managed assets, and is diversified across 317 holdings. The CEF maintains a 51%/49% split between REIT common equity and non-REIT preferred securities, to blend the best of both worlds into one packaged financial instrument. RNP’s REIT segment is dominantly composed of the best-in-class REITs, representing ~29.8% of the invested assets. Source.

Telecom, healthcare, data centers, industrials, and self-storage properties represent ~56% of the REIT portion of the investment, making it quite resilient to economic pressures.

On the other side, we have a non-REIT preferred stock portfolio, with the top issuers being banking, utility, insurance, and pipeline companies, representing ~94% of this section. These issuers are tightly regulated and pay qualified dividends, increasing the tax-advantaged composition of RNP. The CEF operates with a ~31% leverage, with 81% fixed at a weighted average rate of 2% for a weighted average period of 2.1 years. This is notably different from our December report, where the leverage was 81% fixed at a 1.7% rate for 1.8 years. So, RNP was able to extend the debt maturity with only a modest increase in borrowing cost, indicating its strong borrowing potential at times of elevated interest rates.

RNP has been delivering income for shareholders since its inception in 2003, massively outperforming what the market indices can over the past two decades, including the Great Financial Crisis, which was particularly detrimental for its core holdings (real estate and financial services firms). We also note that over the past five years, RNP has averaged +85% tax-advantaged distributions.

In 2024, about 40% of the CEF’s distributions were funded by NII (Net Investment Income), which comprises dividend and interest income, and the rest was funded by realized capital gains. RNP is actively managed, and we expect the fund manager to actively rotate between holdings to maximize these gains, and with rate cuts upon us, this asset class is well-positioned to deliver further price upside.

RNP pays monthly distributions reflecting a 7.5% annualized yield, and presents a compelling investment at the cusp of more rate cuts to come.

Pick #2: DMB – Yield 4.5%

The last time we wrote about BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund, Inc. (DMB), we wrote:

"DMBs' distribution is relatively low. As NAV recovers, we expect that DMB will join peers who have already raised their dividends. As we are entering into a period where there could be turmoil in the markets and the risk of a recession is high, having a fund that is likely to start raising its distribution and have upside potential when everything else is falling is worth its weight in gold."

DMB is a municipal bond fund, investing primarily in self-funding Muni-bonds which are often tied to dedicated cashflows. In May, DMB raised its dividend 28% to $0.038/month. We view this as just the start of a distribution raising cycle as DMB is still paying out just 4% of NAV, while many peers like Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD) are paying out +7%. There is ample room for DMB to continue increasing its distribution. Despite the last distribution hike, DMB continues to trade at about a 10% discount to NAV:

We believe that DMB is capable of trading at a discount of 5% or less as their distribution rises to be more competitive with peers. DMB's interest rate sensitivity is to the long end of the interest rate curve.

DMB owns assets with long maturities, with over 55% of its portfolio having a maturity of 20+ years, and over 89% of its portfolio having over 10 years from maturity. Source.

This means that the assets DMB holds will see prices move with long-term Treasury prices. If 10 to 30-year Treasuries trade at a lower yield, DMB's assets will go up in price, and if 10 (US10Y) to 30-year (US30Y) Treasuries trade at higher yields, DMB's assets will tend to go down in price. The main driver of DMB's decline in NAV in recent years has been the increase in long-term Treasury rates.

Going forward, how much and how quickly DMB's NAV recovers will greatly depend on what long-term Treasury rates do.

In terms of the dividend, higher rates are ultimately positive as interest from bonds is what drives cash flow. Higher rates mean higher yields.

Like many CEFs, DMB uses leverage to amplify returns. Currently, its leverage is approximately 36%. This leverage is at a floating rate, which has been a headwind to cash flow for many bond CEFs. The less DMB pays in interest expense on its leverage, the more cash is available to be retained for growing NAV or distributed to investors.

Typically, short-term interest rates are lower than long-term interest rates. However, over the past couple of years, we saw short-term rates shoot up, and that meant higher interest expense for DMB. We've seen the inversion come to an end:

To the extent that the Fed cuts interest rates again, that will increase cash flow for DMB. Whether it causes a rally in NAV is less certain, as it is perfectly possible for short-term rates to come down while long-term rates go up. We've seen that so far in 2025.

Either way, it's a net positive for DMB because cash flow will go up if interest expense comes down, even if their NAV stays low.

Municipal bonds offer investors tax-efficient exposure to high-quality debt, while collecting a healthy yield. DMB's portfolio is primarily investment grade:

This makes it a powerful diversifier for a portfolio like ours, where our credit risk tends to be exposure to companies with credits in the B-BB range.

Long-term interest rates are at multi-decade highs; it is a gift from the market to be able to buy at these low prices and enjoy distribution growth as the rate curve steepens.

Conclusion

RNP and DMB are two very interesting funds to hold. RNP is well-established as a strong income-generating option, combining well-known and well-loved assets. DMB, however, is less known and less loved. Its managers were quick to reduce the distribution to protect the fund's NAV, but slow to hike it, again with a focus on NAV development vs. shareholder rewards. Now, it's coming back and raising the distribution again, fueled by the NAV they carefully cultivated to greater strengths.

When retirement arrives, you'll have a lot of change with it. It's an exciting but also life-altering paradigm shift. My goal for everyone reading my articles is for them to have the retirement they always dreamed of. It will be as unique as each of you, but there are many common issues and hurdles to face. Don't let your finances rob you of your dreams. Let them be the fuel driving you to success.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Affirm: 40 Is My Entry Point
data.description:
According to the reverse DCF executed in this article, Affirm’s premium is too high for me to go long for now. I’m looking at 40 as a good entry point. Despite being the leader, while having multiple growth levers, its asset heavy business and high leverage impact negatively the company thesis.

Nevertheless, I consider Affirm a good buy at certain levels due to the effectiveness of BNPL, the massive market potential of this solution, and Affirm being one of the major winners in this large future market.

Background

Affirm (NASDAQ:AFRM) is a BNPL enabler that is achieving considerable network effects, having a lot of popularity among the largest retailers and American consumers. BNPL is an interesting industry, especially in the US, but in other countries as well.

US consumers are very used to using debt to fuel their life expenses, compared to the European counterparts. Starting with very expensive college funding, that most of the time leads to a new job outside of the student’s home state. Again, this impacts debt levels because some of these new workers are going to buy a new house with debt.

Looking at the numbers from the New York Fed, Household debt keeps increasing. From 2008 to 2024, the debt levels increased around 50%. Of course, we need to consider GDP growth and the relation between debt and GDP. Nevertheless, debt levels are increasing, and are not showing signs of slowing down.

The debt cycle is vicious, leading to the USA reporting considerable personal bankruptcies per capita. Due to the debt accumulated, it is hard for most Americans to save. In fact, 54% have savings for 3 months of expenses. This means that, even well of Americans have to use credit for travelling, medical procedures, consumer electronics and luxury items.

The market for BNPL, first in the USA, and then in the rest of the world, has a lot of potential for long-term gains. Consumers are attracted to cards and merchants that allow them to buy more up-front with credit, paying interest after.

The data, from the Affirm’s Amazon’s partnership, suggests that BNPL increases ticket size and fuels loyalty. Amazon’s Affirm BNPL transactions are 60% larger than normal transactions and 88% of customers are repeat BNPL buyers.

Strategy

BNPL has proven to be an effective alternative to credit cards, providing better economics for the merchants who join the service, like we have seen from the Amazon's and Affirm's partnership. This creates competition for the massive 5.51 trillion dollar credit card USA market.

The BNPL industry itself is very competitive, with multiple fintechs providing the services, alongside large merchants working on their own BNPL solutions.

These fintechs position their services for different consumers segments while using different types of loans. There should be space for multiple companies and different strategies. But, one thing is certain, this is a winner takes most market.

For example, Sezzle (SEZL) differs from Affirm. It has a much lighter balance sheet, because it doesn’t focus on doing long-term loans to its users. Instead, it provides mostly 0% pay-in-four loans. The previous loans, usually, are of a smaller ticket size. Consequently, this company can loan to more risky users. Contrasting to the higher quality, long-term loans generated by Affirm.

Having said this, Affirm should be executing its strategy according to this statement.

Quickly growth the number of relationships with customers and merchants, achieving the desired network effects. Network effects are very important for BNPL companies. The more customers with strong relations to the BNPL company (using its app multiple times a week), the more merchants are willing to join the BNPL service. In addition, the more merchants use the BNPL service, the more customers are willing to join the fintech.

Additionally, if Affirm is able to become a very large company, it will have better debt terms (too big to fail).

When I’m looking at Affirm stock, I’m not really worried about profitability, but I’m looking for expansion in the number of active merchants and in its active Affirm card users. Which has been great for now.

For example, in May 2025, if we look at Seeking Alpha's press releases from the Affirm stock, we can see that it executed around 6 partnerships. I'm highlighting the Costco partnership as the most important.

It is also important to understand how Affirm will manage a medium downturn and a large downturn. The latter hasn’t happened yet, thus, is a major risk for the stock.

The value of Affirm’s network is ultimately a function of the number of relationships we have with consumers and merchants – directly or through a strategic partner – and we spend all available time maximizing this number: always thoughtfully, and also aggressively when it makes sense.

2025 Q3 - Latest Shareholder Letter

Financials

Affirm gross margins have increased YoY despite 0% APR monthly installments growth of 44% year over year. These short-term loans have lower gross margins than the typical pay in 1-3 years loans, that carry interest payments. Gross margins have also increased YoY in a period when interest rates set by the FED are unchanged. This leaves some gross margin upside in an eventual interest rate decrease.

The consumer interest rates, charged by Affirm, are not as sensitive to the Fed's set interest rate (for example, the merchant fees are fixed), but the interest charged to Affirm is. In the future, if inflation or the economy slows down, we might see Affirm receiving a nice boost in gross profit from the Fed's rate decreasing.

Affirm, compared to other companies like Sezzle (SEZL), it is much more invested in long-term consumer loans. Its long-term investments, mostly in loans made to consumers, occupy most of the Employed Capital.

This is an asset-heavy business. In order to grow, Affirm has to increase employed capital by large amounts. Consequently, reducing dividends, buybacks, money for acquisitions.

Despite this, I prefer Affirm network effects compared to Sezzle business, which has a much lighter balance sheet and is much more profitable.

Looking at the net debt, Affirm’s FCF in the TTM period takes 6 years to pay the net debt. Because of this, Affirm is a considerable-leveraged play.

Opportunities

The company will grow in the future with FCF increasing by a large amount. This makes the debt burden more manageable. One of the main growth opportunities is the Affirm card.

There are just 2M active cardholders, with the potential of achieving a much higher active card membership number in the short term. If we consider that there were 40 million Affirm registered users in 2023, there should be a high number of users with relations with Affirm looking to join the Affirm's card.

If we look at the historical growth of the Affirm card, we can understand that the card active users growth has been decreasing. But it keeps growing at an excellent pace. In 2025 Q1 the YoY growth of the active card users was of 136%, reaching 1.7 million. In 2025 Q3 the number of active users, as mentioned, was 2 million. If we compare it to the number of active users in 2024 Q3 (1 million active users), we can calculate an excellent but decelerating growth of 100%.

Affirm card allows the customer to pay with debit and credit, being versatile for day-to-day purchases. The card enables the customer to use BNPL services across every merchant that accepts Visa.

This service will boost the number of long-term loans and reduce its risk by gathering more information about the customers’ transactions.

Speaking of the Affirm Card, the power tool for Affirm consumers had another strong quarter – $807 million of GMV (115% year-over-year growth) from approximately 2M active cardholders. The team has launched countless features making the card better (and adding hundreds of millions of projected annual GMV to the program): expanding card availability to more Affirm consumers, increasing onboarding success, adding seamless foreign transaction support, and relentlessly improving user experience. We continue to see excellent retention and usage from our cardholders, and are investing significant efforts in making their overall card experience better.

2025 Q3 - Latest Shareholder Letter

Risks

Affirm hasn’t yet suffered a large and prolonged financial meltdown like the 2008 crisis, that had a huge impact on liquidity. Companies that were funding themselves with short-term commercial paper had liquidity issues, not being able to reroll the short-term debt that was supported by long-term loans payments.

Interbank lending was also heavily affected, further putting pressure on a significantly leveraged system, with assets/debts duration mismatched.

Nowadays, companies are more conservative with their funding practices. In this case, Affirm doesn't use short-term commercial paper to fund itself. Banks also have more guardrails. A new regulation was introduced, in 2008, that states the need to comply with certain liquidity ratios.

Affirm finances its loans using mostly bank partners and long-term debt. The company’s revenue generation loans have short duration. Thus, the company shouldn’t face many liquidity issues.

Still, if there is another systemic financial crisis, Affirm could see its funding disappear, impacting revenue growth by not generating incremental loans. Costs would be impacted by the default of the consumer-driven loans and increased loan interest.

Most of Affirm’s business is related to interest revenue. In fact, it jumped 2x from 2023 to 2024. Recently, resulting in more than half of Affirm's revenue. Affirm loan sales revenue is just around 10% of the total interest revenue. This shows that the company's business model has significant exposure to a new financial crisis, or an economic slowdown, due to the large number of loans in its portfolio.

Valuation

In the next reverse DCF, where I obtain the revenue growth by considering the current price, I’m using as inputs a discount rate of 11% and a terminal growth rate of 4%.

To the average SP500 return, I add 1% to account for the risk of a high debt structure and the ongoing tariff war. Some could suggest 11% is a low discount rate for Affirm, given the circumstances, but I’m willing to trust in Affirm’s risk management.

For the terminal growth rate, I consider 4% fair because Affirm's revenue is very associated with the growth in US consumer spending. 4% seems a good rate of growth, even in the long term, for this category.

The TTM FCF margin is 20%. So, to be conservative, I consider a 17% FCF margin in the model.

I also account for dilution, which is substantial in Affirm’s case. In the calculations, I’m using a 3.5% dilution of share count across the years.

The model outputs a required growth rate of above 20% for the next ten years. This is a very high-quality company, defining the new ways of consuming, which makes the premium very high. Still, I consider the combination of premium, financial mechanics, opportunities and risks not sufficiently attractive to me.

Conclusion

Affirm is building the desired network effects to win most of the market. Affirm card and the improvement of the machine learning models are good opportunities for the business to increase revenue growth.

There are negatives. The company is a very asset heavy business. To grow it has to significantly increase the balance sheet. Most well priced software stocks don’t show this condition.

The company also has a very high premium. If it goes under 40, still at a premium, I’m willing to buy it. According to my reverse DCF model, 40 is the price that outputs a consistent growth rate of around 20% for the rest of the decade. For now, I rate it as a hold.

Market Review

  • U.S. fixed income markets were mixed in the first quarter. The period began on strong footing as U.S. macroeconomic data indicated robust consumer and labor markets, and 2025 GDP growth forecasted at 2.1%. Credit markets were the big winner at the beginning of the year, with spreads grinding to their tightest levels since 2007. However, the quarter ended on a weaker note, and spreads ended the quarter wider. Growth fears, tariff uncertainty, and concerns over the new administration’s policy agenda were major drivers of this weakness, in addition to softer macroeconomic data, disinflation worries, and cracks in the AI growth narrative. This led to a meaningful decline in Treasury yields across the curve, leading to a boost in U.S. Treasuries and longer-duration assets over the second half of the quarter.
  • While there were reports of declines in consumer sentiment and confidence, hard data like nonfarm payrolls, core Consumer Price Index (CPI), and retail sales held up relatively well throughout the quarter. Additionally, other positive signs emerged, such as resilient consumer spending and better-than-expected February economic data. The labor market remained strong, and the March Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting offered dovish takeaways, helping to mitigate some of the negative sentiment.
  • Over the quarter, investment grade corporate 2 spreads widened 15bps, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads widened 11 bps, and asset-backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads widened by 16 bps. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield decreased from 4.24% to 3.89% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield decreased from 4.57% to 4.21%.

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned 2.62%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended March 31st, 2025. The Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index*, returned 2.78% during the same period.
  • Duration positioning detracted from relative performance over the quarter. Being underweight duration versus the benchmark detracted as U.S. Treasury rates moved lower during the period.
  • An allocation to high yield corporate bonds also detracted from relative returns. We modestly increased the portfolio’s overall exposure to high-yield corporates.
  • There were no other significant detractors over the period.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds contributed to relative performance, specifically within the Financials sector. We increased the portfolio’s overall exposure to investment grade corporate bonds as we believe they offer attractive relative value.
  • The portfolio’s allocation to ABS and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) modestly contributed to relative performance. Within CLOs and ABS, we decreased the portfolio’s overall exposure, specifically within AAA-rated.
  • The portfolio’s allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) contributed to relative performance. We believe this is an attractive asset class for high-quality carry, and it may perform well as rate volatility declines.

data.title: The Retiree's Taxable Dividend, April 2025 Portfolio Review: The Case For Maintaining Modest Cash On Hand
data.description:
Having some extra cash on hand can be really helpful during turbulent times, especially when you don't need to use that cash to pay bills or other basic necessities. Some investors prefer to live where every dollar is invested, and while that may return more dividend income, it doesn't create opportunities to capitalize during market downturns.

We capitalized on several purchases in the Taxable Account during the month of April as nearly every stock presented an attractive entry point. We chose to capitalize by adding to existing positions with the following stocks:

As of the market close on May 23rd, all the shares purchased are sitting above their original purchase price. The same also goes for Simon Property Group (SPG) and Rithm Capital (RITM) which were both trimmed back during this time. These were two companies that I was more concerned with when it came to the downside risk that the tariff announcement presented.

The loss associated with SPG was close to break-even, while the loss on RITM was much larger than you would normally see. One reason why we were more ok taking this loss is because of this being a Taxable Account, and therefore we can use the loss to offset gains from the sale of shares in 2025.

If we total up the purchases and remove the funds generated from stocks we sold we executed approximately $8K worth of trades that all turned out favorably.

It's fair to point out that John and Jane carry a substantial amount of cash on hand in their portfolios, but we believe that it is worth it when we are able to capitalize on situations like this. Another reason John and Jane carry more cash on hand is because we want to provide a buffer in the event that they need to draw emergency funds (doing this prevents the need to sell shares, which would adversely impact cash flow).

April Dividend Increases

During the month of April, five companies increased their dividend payout or provided a special dividend.

  • EPR Properties (EPR) - Dividend increased by 3.5%
  • Iron Mountain (IRM) - Dividend increased by 9.8%
  • Realty Income (O) - Dividend increased by .2%
  • Schlumberger (SLB) - Dividend increased by 3.6%
  • WP Carey (WPC) - Dividend increased by 1.1%

April Trades

I showed the trades that took place in the introduction but wanted to take a closer look at a few of them.

Nordson (NDSN) — Earnings were just reported with a beat on earnings, revenue, and better than-expected outlook. We added shares at $172 because NDSN is the best-of-breed company with a track record of major earnings and dividend growth. With a payout ratio of 30% and a 5-year growth rate of 15.2%, Nordson makes sense to buy, especially when the rare situation happens that the stock price corrects. Even though the share price didn't reach a level as low as what was seen during early 2020, the dividend growth has been so tremendous that the yield reached a 10-year high.

Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Production Partners (EPD) — Both of these MLPs saw buying opportunities materialize and with an already low-cost basis, it made sense that even more shares. The Master Limited Partnership (MLP) sector has made a major comeback over the last several years, with significant setbacks in 2016 and 2020. These positions are already sizable holdings in the taxable portfolio, but the risk/reward at the price they were purchased justifies adding more.

Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) — ADM is a boring business that has experienced several setbacks over the last few years but has continued to grow its dividend even as concerns over earnings growth and accounting concerns have dropped the stock price to almost half of its value a few short years ago. UBS recently upgraded ADM citing that the bad news is fully priced in, which is why we added when shares dropped into the low $40/share range.

Texas Instruments (TXN) is definitely not a strong buy at current prices, but it was definitely worth adding to under $150/share with a yield of over 3.50%. Although dividend growth has slowed down in recent years, it still carries a 10-year dividend growth rate of 15% with a five-year dividend growth rate of 9%. Although the dividend matches the estimated earnings, it doesn't take into account the explosive earnings growth estimates for the next few years.

Over the last 10 years, TXN has been able to grow its dividend by 15% annually, reduce its outstanding shares by approximately 10%, and effectively triple the stock price. While some could argue the best days are behind it, I still think there is plenty of opportunity as long as shares are purchased at the right point. As you can see below, since 2020 when the share closes in on $150/share, has acted as a strong support level.

Portfolio Composition

The images below are focused on what is happening now and moving forward.

The first image shows what has happened year-over-year with the portfolio in terms of what holdings are generating income.

Below is an updated table of the extremely conservative forecast that suggests income will be up 4.7% in FY-2025, which is up from March's estimate of 4.1%. Although income for the month of April was down year-over-year the reason, we saw the FY number increase was because we had estimated income for the month was going to be even less than it was in our original estimates.

The next image has been updated from my forecasting articles to look at how much the income has grown on a monthly basis and separate out what income is from dividends and what comes from CDs/Money Markets, etc.

This offers more insight/context as to how the taxable account income is being derived (equities, fixed income, etc.). I started this table because I wanted to give more realistic numbers of what we expect to see for income growth, and it also gives us the ability to better track which months have the largest impact.

Here is a basic understanding of what each of the columns/fields means:

  • 2024 - Income results for 2024
  • 2025 (Ex CDs) - Estimated income 2025 (yellow) and actual income 2025 (green) but excludes all income earned from money markets, CDs, etc.
  • Special Dividend - provides context for irregular or one-time cash flows that distort the income number.
  • 2025 (W CDs) - Estimated CD & money market income 2024 (yellow) and actual income 2025 (green). Separated because we want to differentiate between dividend income and growth compared to CDs/money markets that are not subject to increases.
  • Total Income - Combined total income from dividends, CDs, Money market, etc.
  • 3%/5%/7% Increase - These columns serve as a marker so you can see how much income would need to be hit that month to achieve a specific increase. I will likely change these numbers to be more account-specific in the future (some accounts are growing more rapidly than others).

It's also important to remember that John and Jane have been taking a monthly paycheck from their taxable Account since the start of 2022. This is also a factor that contributed to faster income growth early on, since all the money was being collected as cash and reinvested.

The current amount being drawn is $1,700/month, compared to an estimated FY-2025 estimated monthly cash flow of $2,064.06/month. This means that every month, the taxable Account generates an average of $364.06 more cash flow than what John and Jane draw from it. The income generated has been sustainable this entire time, which means that John and Jane's withdrawals are not coming from the sale of stock to generate the proceeds.

One reason why John and Jane don't draw as much as they could is because of the distortion created by special dividends like the one paid by Old Republic International (ORI) in January.

Conclusion

With the special dividend included, John and Jane's Taxable Account is trending towards year-over-year income growth in the high single digits to low double digits. With the special dividend removed, they are trending towards low-to-mid single-digit income growth.

The account balance has dropped over the last few months, but even as I write this article the account balance going into the end of May will likely be up around $15K compared to the end of April.

Even with this pullback, the average balance of the portfolio is still trending well above the average balance in FY-2024. I am willing to acknowledge that using the balance at the end of the month for an average is overly simplistic, but it works as a basic tool for measuring changes in trends and is helpful for comparing monthly balances year-over-year.

Keep the ideas coming in the comment section of any attractive individual stocks/ETFs/Closed-End Funds that are worth considering for income investors.

data.title: IVV: S&P 500 Dashboard For June

This monthly article reports sector metrics in the S&P 500 Index, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze ETFs tracking this index. Among them, iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) is second behind SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) for dollar volume and third behind Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and SPY for assets under management. IVV has a lower expense ratio than SPY: only 0.03%.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios in every sector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). All are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all these ratios, higher is better and negative is bad. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also calculate two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians rather than averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. Capital-weighted averages are skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. As a consequence, these metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

Historical baselines are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh. For example, the value of EYh for technology in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield of S&P 500 tech companies.

The Value Score "VS" is the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score "QS" is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance, except in energy and utilities where the Free Cash Flow Yield is ignored to avoid some inconsistencies. VS and QS are capped between -100 and +100 when the calculation goes beyond these values.

Score variations since last month:

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns by sector.

Interpretation

A hypothetical S&P 500 “median” company is overvalued by about 10% relative to average valuation metrics since 2013. The quality score is slightly above the historical baseline. We can translate median yields in their inverse ratios:

Price/Earnings: 24.94 - Price/Sales: 2.94 - Price/Free Cash Flow: 42.74

Energy has been showing the best value and quality scores among GICS sectors since February 2022. Healthcare and communication services are undervalued by about 16% and 11% relative to 11-year averages, respectively. Consumer staples, real estate, consumer discretionary and financials are close to their historical baseline in valuation. Utilities are overvalued by 14% based on the same metrics. Industrials, technology and materials are overvalued by over 20%.

The S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:IVV) has gained 13.3% in 12 months, while the median return is 11% (reported in the table above) and the equal-weight average is at 8.2% (measured on RSP). It means the index performance has been moderately skewed to the upside by mega-cap companies over the last 12 months. The S&P mid-cap 400 index and the small cap index Russell 2000 have been lagging the large cap index (1.8% for MDY and 0.8% for IWM).

We use the table above to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells that a large consumer staples company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0468 (or price/earnings below 21.37) is in the better half of the sector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list of 71 stocks sent to subscribers for the month of June 2025. It is a rotational model with a positive bias on the long-term, not the result of in-depth stock analysis.

data.title: Armada Hoffler: The Knife Has Fallen, Time To Pick It Up
data.description:
My previous article on Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH) was issued on February 2025 before the dividend cut was announced. The title of the article was: Armada Hoffler Properties: Catching A Falling Knife Or Diamond? I'm Still Long. As the title indicates, I was rather bullish on AHH. The main driver for my bullishness was the assumption of lower base rates and the associated tailwinds that should help AHH gradually emerge out of a very tight dividend coverage level. Plus, the underlying business seemed rather robust.

However, since the publication of my piece, the stock is down by ~20%.

The dividend cut in combination with additional equity raise and weaker than expected AFFO guidance gave the market a strong reason to assign even more aggressive risk premiums to AHH's valuation. Plus, the volatility in the long-term yields and the tariff-induced uncertainty have not helped AHH either.

Now, I think that by looking at the title of the current article, it should be clear what my stance is on AHH. I know that the ~20% decline after my previous thesis is not encouraging and does not necessarily provide a confidence that this time the total return will be acceptable.

I fully get it.

Having said that, let me still articulate my thinking on why, in my view, AHH presents a very compelling risk to reward play.

Thesis Review

Let's start with the important basics.

Because of the massive share price decline, but actually stable FFO generation, the P/FFO multiple has dropped to 6.7x, which is almost perfectly in line with the hotel REIT sector median multiple. The thing is that hotel REITs tend to be the lowest priced players in the entire REIT universe because their cash flows are inherently volatile (i.e., there are no long-term lease agreements) and extremely sensitive to the business cycles. In fact, currently, we are in a period of uncertainty, where the sentiment is negative. This means that the hotel REIT multiple is lower than average. And even considering these nuances, there is no difference between AHH's multiple and that of relatively depressed hotel REITs.

Furthermore, the forward dividend yield for AHH stands at 7.9%. This is quite high despite the recent dividend cut by ~32%, which has normalized the AFFO payout to ~70%.

Again, the reason for this attractive yield lies in the combination of dirt cheap multiple and strong cash flow generation.

The question is whether the AFFO generation per share is stable. If we can conclude that the AFFO is actually stable, then this fact alone should make the case attractive enough for a buy (e.g., rich dividend with a notable multiple recovery potential).

Here are my three key reasons why I think that the AFFO per share could be deemed sustainable.

First, the underlying business remains in a solid shape. For example, in Q1 2025 figures we can see that the overall portfolio had an occupancy ratio of 95% across all three segments (office, retail and high-multiple multifamily). For many, the office segment is what causes a concern. Yet, exactly this segment had the best occupancy level at 97.5%, and there are no meaningful lease expirations through 2027.

Apart from the occupancy aspect, the total portfolio NOI increased by 2% on a year-over-year basis, which is certainly not indicative of a declining asset base. Plus, and this is important to appreciate even though it might seem as a small detail, the Q1 2025 AHH's operational cost base has decreased by 13% compared to the same period last year.

As a result of stable occupancy, steadily growing NOI and improved cost base, the AFFO per share in Q1 landed at $0.20, which is by $0.02 higher than in the prior quarter.

Second, the capital structure remains aggressive. While the stabilized debt to EBITDA is 5.4x (i.e., a conservative statistic), the total or consolidated metric is 7.1x, which is quite high. However, I do not think that this should be a reason to avoid AHH, especially considering the following mitigants:

  • The AFFO payout is 70%, which means that 30% of the cash generation is kept at the REIT's books and could be used for debt reduction (i.e., we can already see a progress here).
  • The fact that the AFFO per share is advancing higher translates to a larger cash flow retention base that could also be used for debt reduction activity. I really doubt that AHH will increase the dividend in the foreseeable future, which is totally fine given where the current yield is.
  • The consensus indicates that the base rates are set to go down in the near to medium-term, which means that there will be some positive boost to the AFFO generation through a reduced interest cost burden (although I would not put a huge expectation/bet here).

Speaking of AFFO stability, another nuance that we have to factor in is AHH's lease profile. Namely, this year and in 2026, AHH has only 3% and 4%, respectively, of total leases expiring. This provides another tailwind for slowly but surely reinvesting the retained AFFO into the balance sheet optimization program.

Third, the consensus market expectation indicates the following FFO per share trajectory:

2025 - FFO per share of $1.05.

2026 - FFO per share of $1.06.

2027 - FFO per share of $1.16.

Again, it seems that the analysts agree with me that the underlying business is growing. What these figures also imply is that the cash flow retention levels will gradually go up, which, as articulated above, should offer a notable support for the management in working down the total debt load that is currently on the aggressive side.

Finally, if we now turn back to the valuation aspect. I think that by digesting the aforementioned data and facts, we can safely conclude that AHH is not falling off the cliff. The business is stable (e.g., occupancy high, NOI growing, favorable lease expiry profile) and the dividend is well-covered.

Now, let's also consider this:

  • AHH's P/FFO multiple is 6.7x.
  • The REIT segment category (diversified) in which AHH is placed has an average multiple of 10.0x.
  • 13.5% of AHH's leases stem from multifamily, which has an average multiple of 20.9x.
  • 25% of AHH's leases are placed in retail segment, which has an average multiple of 13.7x.
  • Then the rest goes to the category that is called "mixed use", which consists of a combination of multifamily, retail and office. And the office REIT segment has an average multiple of 9.4x.

Everywhere we look, AHH is severely discounted. While I do not have an exact estimate for what the justified multiple could exactly be, but my base case is that if AHH manages to prove the market that the AFFO per share is sustainable and even slightly growing, then the multiple should revert back to the low double-digit level (or at minimum to the office-level multiple).

Key Risks

There are obviously some risks involved in the case here. The market is not that irrational. Namely, there is a reason why AHH is heavily discounted (although I do not agree with the level of discount).

While I do not see any material risks on the property side due to the resilient operational performance (e.g., healthy occupancy, like-for-like NOI growth), I am a bit worried about the leverage.

As articulated above, a debt to EBITDA of 7.1x is high. So, there is a very tiny margin of error embedded in the books. And the real risk here lies in the refinancing dynamic, where if the long-term yields rise, AHH will have to inevitably experience notable headwinds to the AFFO generation, which potentially might even lead to an AFFO contraction.

However, there are two reasonable mitigants in place:

  • The debt maturity profile is laddered, meaning that the bulk of maturities is back-end loaded (after 2026).
  • The current weighted average interest rate is 4.2%, which is not that far off from the AHH on-the-run term loan facility rates (~6.3%).

The Bottom-Line

AHH is a high risk REIT, no question about that.

Yet, we have to put all of this into the context of dirt-cheap multiple, well-covered and high dividend and a growing core business.

In my view, the risk and reward profile is attractive, providing a decent basis for investors to go long.

In a nutshell, I remain bullish on AHH.

data.title: Alphabet Stock Is A Value Trap
data.description:
Right now, I am seeing increasingly many value investors pitch Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (TSX:GOOG:CA) as a great investment opportunity. Nearly everyone has a "Strong Buy" rating for it on Seeking Alpha.

Its share price has recently sold off, even as the company kept growing its cash flow. As a result, it now trades at a historically low valuation:

Current P/E 10-year Average P/E
Alphabet 19x 26x

Here, you can also see its historic P/E multiple in a chart. You can clearly see that it is historically low:

Despite being so cheap, as I write this, analysts are still projecting the company to grow its earnings per share by about 12% through 2029.

So what's not to like?

Low valuation, coupled with rapid growth, is a recipe for high returns. Add some multiple expansion to the growth, and you could double your money within the next 5 years.

Even then, I am not buying it.

On the contrary, this is one of the last tech stocks that I would dare to buy in today's rapidly evolving world, and it all comes down to my personal experience as a user of Google.

I have replaced 90%+ of my Google searches with AIs like ChatGPT, and I don't see myself going back.

Why This Is Very Negative for Google

First off, you need to recognize that search is still Google's golden goose.

Yes, it has greatly diversified its business over the past decades with YouTube, Google Play, Android, Chrome, Cloud, Waymo, and other smaller bets.

But Search still makes up about 50% of Alphabet's revenue, and since this is a high-margin business, it makes up closer to 2/3 of its profits.

Therefore, if users like me shift away from Google search, its main profit engine will be at risk.

And this is happening right now.

AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Grok are far more efficient at finding the right information than Google.

Instead of having to click on 10 links, you get answers right away.

They are replacing the process of "searching" with direct answers. It is far faster and more efficient in most cases.

I think that I am just a bit ahead of the crowd, but I expect most people to move in this direction and gradually replace "searching" with AI assistants.

Here, you might say that Alphabet is also developing its own AI tools, and you are right. Google is one of the leaders in AI.

But here's the issue:

AI Will Cannibalize Their Search Business

The Google search business is so insanely profitable because of the whole process of "searching."

You are clicking 10 links to find the right information, and you come across countless ads in the process, printing cash for Google.

But if Google goes all in on AI, it will cannibalize its most profitable business.

People will search far less since they are getting direct answers with AI assistants. This results in far fewer impressions and less ad inventory.

Therefore, even if Google is successful at building AI tools, it still won't be able to monetize them as efficiently as its Search business.

There is also no guarantee that they will even succeed. Their AI tools are promising, but monetizing them may also prove challenging given that so many of them are completely free.

Bottom Line: Google is in a Tough Spot

They have two options:

They can stick with search and hope that AI assistants won't get adopted to the same degree as I am already using them.

Or they can go all in on AI, but this will hurt their most profitable business.

In a way, they find themselves in a similar situation to Kodak when digital photography took off, and they realized that it would kill their film photography business.

There is no good solution.

Both options will likely lead to much lower profits over time. I strongly believe that Google search will fall behind in AI adoption because I have already changed my habits, and it is far better this way.

It will force them to invest most heavily in AI, but they will struggle with monetization because you don't click on 5 ads when you are in an AI chatbot. If they overwhelm you with ads, you will simply use another AI tool like ChatGPT.

So, how I see it is that Google has lost its monopoly. AI tools are replacing search, and this is a real existential crisis for the company.

With that in mind, I have no interest in buying shares of the company.

I am not a short seller, but if I were looking for a tech company to short, Google is one I would consider. The valuation multiple is likely still too high, and so are the analyst estimates for future growth.
